engineering.translation: The implementation rule, restated in metaphysical language. Those deluded by the gunas (guṇa-sammūḍhāḥ) are attached to the actions of the gunas. The kṛtsna-vit (knower of the whole) should not unsettle these akṛtsna-vidaḥ (knowers of the part), the mandān (the dull). The verse extends 3.26 with the philosophical justification: the partial-knower's attachment to action is the proper modality for his state, and unsettling him produces drift. In engineering: the senior who has internalised the system's metaphysics — that the team's collective practice produces the output, that individual authorship is a misreading — should not preach this to the junior who is still building the discipline of personal accountability. The junior's framework — 'I own this; I shipped this; I am responsible' — is exactly what they need at their stage; destabilising it produces an engineer who has neither the personal-accountability framework nor the realised seeing that exempts from it.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A principal engineer at a Postgres-shaped marketplace reads a junior's retrospective in Notion. The retrospective is in 'I shipped this' framing — claiming kartāham at the operational layer. The principal could correct this. She could leave a comment naming the five contributors the junior failed to credit, citing the invisible work that produced the artifact, redirecting his framework toward the kṛtsna-vit reading. She does not. She sees that the junior is still building the personal-accountability discipline that, two years from now, will be the foundation of his kṛtsna-vit shift. Telling him 'you didn't actually ship that — the system shipped that' would unsettle the framework he is constructing without giving him the realised state that would make destabilising productive. Instead she leaves a remark praising specific decisions and quietly mentions, in a one-on-one with his manager, 'this junior is at the stage where I shipped this is the right framework; we'll know we have him at the next stage when he begins writing we shipped this without prompting.' The verse names this exactly: kṛtsna-vit na vicālayet — the knower of the whole should not unsettle.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if read as 'never give feedback to juniors.' The verse is naming a specific failure mode: destabilising the partial-knower's working framework without offering the framework that would replace it. Feedback that grows the junior's framework is not vicālayet; signaling that their framework is naive is.
engineering.counter_example: Some juniors are ready for the kṛtsna-vit shift earlier. The senior who senses this readiness can introduce the more accurate authorship-reading and the junior will integrate it productively. The verse's force is on the case where the junior is not yet ready and the senior's signaling produces drift instead of growth. The diagnostic is whether the watcher integrates the new framework or merely loses the old one.
engineering.implication: When you are tempted to correct a junior's kartāham framing, ask: am I giving them the kṛtsna-vit framework, or am I just pulling away the framework they currently have? If only the latter, the verse names the move as wrong.
engineering.quotable_line: Pulling away the junior's framework without giving them the next one produces drift. The verse names this and forbids it.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation for BG 3.29: source pack triangulated across 5 sources; engineering layer anchored on chapter thesis (Why the Senior Still Ships) and load-bearing claims for this verse.
